texts.label: section_header
texts.orig: Designing Stochastic Channels
texts.text: Designing Stochastic Channels

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Matthew A. Graydon, 1, 2, ∗ Joshua Skanes-Norman, 1, 3, 4 and Joel J. Wallman 1, 3, 4

texts.orig: (Dated: January 19, 2022)
texts.text: (Dated: January 19, 2022)

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Stochastic channels are ubiquitous in the field of quantum information because they are simple and easy to analyze. In particular, Pauli channels and depolarizing channels are widely studied because they can be efficiently simulated in many relevant quantum circuits. Despite their wide use, the properties of general stochastic channels have received little attention. In this paper, we prove that the diamond distance of a general stochastic channel from the identity coincides with its process infidelity to the identity. We demonstrate with an explicit example that there exist multiqubit stochastic channels that are not unital. We then discuss the relationship between unitary 1-designs and stochastic channels. We prove that the twirl of an arbitrary quantum channel by a unitary 1-design is always a stochastic channel. However, unlike with unitary 2-designs, the twirled channel depends upon the choice of unitary 1-design. Moreover, we prove by example that there exist stochastic channels that cannot be obtained by twirling a quantum channel by a unitary 1-design.
texts.text: Stochastic channels are ubiquitous in the field of quantum information because they are simple and easy to analyze. In particular, Pauli channels and depolarizing channels are widely studied because they can be efficiently simulated in many relevant quantum circuits. Despite their wide use, the properties of general stochastic channels have received little attention. In this paper, we prove that the diamond distance of a general stochastic channel from the identity coincides with its process infidelity to the identity. We demonstrate with an explicit example that there exist multiqubit stochastic channels that are not unital. We then discuss the relationship between unitary 1-designs and stochastic channels. We prove that the twirl of an arbitrary quantum channel by a unitary 1-design is always a stochastic channel. However, unlike with unitary 2-designs, the twirled channel depends upon the choice of unitary 1-design. Moreover, we prove by example that there exist stochastic channels that cannot be obtained by twirling a quantum channel by a unitary 1-design.

texts.label: section_header
texts.orig: I. INTRODUCTION
texts.text: I. INTRODUCTION

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Quantum computers hold tremendous promise, but noise severely limits the reliability of quantum devices in the noisy intermediate-scale quantum (NISQ) era [1]. Quantum error correction and noise mitigation methods hinge on assumptions about the noise in question [2, 3]. A standard assumption is that the noise is a Pauli channel, because such channels are simple and easy to analyze. In this paper, we consider a more general class of stochastic error processes. A stochastic channel is a completely positive and trace-preserving (CPTP) map admitting a set of Hilbert-Schmidt orthogonal Kraus operators containing a strictly positive multiple of the identity [4]. Operationally, a stochastic channel corresponds to an error occurring with some probability p ∈ [0 , 1). Some nice examples are depolarizing channels, decoherence channels, multi-qubit Pauli channels and qudit Weyl-Heisenberg channels [5]. In this paper, we identify significant operational properties common to all stochastic channels.
texts.text: Quantum computers hold tremendous promise, but noise severely limits the reliability of quantum devices in the noisy intermediate-scale quantum (NISQ) era [1]. Quantum error correction and noise mitigation methods hinge on assumptions about the noise in question [2, 3]. A standard assumption is that the noise is a Pauli channel, because such channels are simple and easy to analyze. In this paper, we consider a more general class of stochastic error processes. A stochastic channel is a completely positive and trace-preserving (CPTP) map admitting a set of Hilbert-Schmidt orthogonal Kraus operators containing a strictly positive multiple of the identity [4]. Operationally, a stochastic channel corresponds to an error occurring with some probability p ∈ [0 , 1). Some nice examples are depolarizing channels, decoherence channels, multi-qubit Pauli channels and qudit Weyl-Heisenberg channels [5]. In this paper, we identify significant operational properties common to all stochastic channels.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: The diamond distance to the identity [6] (hereafter, the diamond distance) is a metric for quantifying error rates in quantum circuits. The diamond distance can be expressed as a semidefinite program [7], but no efficient protocols have been found to measure it directly for arbitrary channels. However, we prove that the diamond distance of a stochastic channel is its process infidelity to the identity (theorem 1); moreover, these measures are simply the probability that an error occurs. The process infidelity to the identity (hereafter, the process infidelity) can be efficiently estimated via randomized benchmarking and generalizations thereof [8-10]. Stochastic channels are thus a class of channels wherein the difficult problem of calculating the diamond distance becomes quite easy.
texts.text: The diamond distance to the identity [6] (hereafter, the diamond distance) is a metric for quantifying error rates in quantum circuits. The diamond distance can be expressed as a semidefinite program [7], but no efficient protocols have been found to measure it directly for arbitrary channels. However, we prove that the diamond distance of a stochastic channel is its process infidelity to the identity (theorem 1); moreover, these measures are simply the probability that an error occurs. The process infidelity to the identity (hereafter, the process infidelity) can be efficiently estimated via randomized benchmarking and generalizations thereof [8-10]. Stochastic channels are thus a class of channels wherein the difficult problem of calculating the diamond distance becomes quite easy.

texts.label: footnote
texts.orig: ∗ m3graydo@uwaterloo.ca
texts.text: ∗ m3graydo@uwaterloo.ca

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Stochastic channels can be created with low overhead. Twirling over unitary t -designs [11-19] is an industry standard technique in quantum information science. A unitary t -design is a normalized measure on the unitary group mimicking its m th Haar moments for all m ≤ t . The twirl of a quantum channel over a unitary 2-design is a depolarizing channel with the same process infidelity. In this paper, we exhibit a similar mechanism with t = 1. We point out that channel twirling commutes with the Choi-Jamiosuppress lkowski isomorphism (lemma 2). We prove that the twirl of any quantum channel over any unitary 1-design is a stochastic channel with p the process fidelity of the input channel (theorem 3). We emphasize that noise can be tailored as such without resorting to some potentially higher unitary t -designs, e.g . Clifford groups [20]. The twirl of a quantum channel over a unitary 1design, however, depends on the chosen unitary 1-design. Many unitary 1-designs exist in all finite dimensions [21].
texts.text: Stochastic channels can be created with low overhead. Twirling over unitary t -designs [11-19] is an industry standard technique in quantum information science. A unitary t -design is a normalized measure on the unitary group mimicking its m th Haar moments for all m ≤ t . The twirl of a quantum channel over a unitary 2-design is a depolarizing channel with the same process infidelity. In this paper, we exhibit a similar mechanism with t = 1. We point out that channel twirling commutes with the Choi-Jamiosuppress lkowski isomorphism (lemma 2). We prove that the twirl of any quantum channel over any unitary 1-design is a stochastic channel with p the process fidelity of the input channel (theorem 3). We emphasize that noise can be tailored as such without resorting to some potentially higher unitary t -designs, e.g . Clifford groups [20]. The twirl of a quantum channel over a unitary 1design, however, depends on the chosen unitary 1-design. Many unitary 1-designs exist in all finite dimensions [21].

texts.label: text
texts.orig: A natural question is whether or not the class of stochastic channels extends beyond the class of mixed unitary channels implemented by unitary operator bases containing the identity. Indeed, the twirl of a quantum channel over a unitary 1-design is unital (theorem 4). We nevertheless establish a positive answer. We define a noisy rank-2 state preparation channel for a system of two qubits that is stochastic but not unital. We also prove that all stochastic qubit channels are unital (theorem 5).
texts.text: A natural question is whether or not the class of stochastic channels extends beyond the class of mixed unitary channels implemented by unitary operator bases containing the identity. Indeed, the twirl of a quantum channel over a unitary 1-design is unital (theorem 4). We nevertheless establish a positive answer. We define a noisy rank-2 state preparation channel for a system of two qubits that is stochastic but not unital. We also prove that all stochastic qubit channels are unital (theorem 5).

texts.label: text
texts.orig: The balance of this paper is structured as follows. In section II, we recall preliminary notions from quantum information theory in order to establish three equivalent definitions of stochastic channels. We then define via Eq. (17) a stochastic channel that is not unital. In section III, we prove that the diamond distance and process infidelity are equal for arbitrary stochastic channels. In section IV, we discuss connections between stochastic channels and unitary 1-designs and we prove lemma 2, theorem 3, and theorem 4. Finally, in section V we conclude and discuss directions for future research.
texts.text: The balance of this paper is structured as follows. In section II, we recall preliminary notions from quantum information theory in order to establish three equivalent definitions of stochastic channels. We then define via Eq. (17) a stochastic channel that is not unital. In section III, we prove that the diamond distance and process infidelity are equal for arbitrary stochastic channels. In section IV, we discuss connections between stochastic channels and unitary 1-designs and we prove lemma 2, theorem 3, and theorem 4. Finally, in section V we conclude and discuss directions for future research.

texts.label: section_header
texts.orig: II. STOCHASTIC CHANNELS
texts.text: II. STOCHASTIC CHANNELS

texts.label: text
texts.orig: We begin by defining stochastic channels, which are informally a noiseless process probabilistically mixed with orthogonal errors. A formal definition of a stochastic channel (in particular, the notion of orthogonal channels) is stated most naturally in terms of the Choi state, which we now review. Let H be a finite-dimensional complex Hilbert space and B ( H ) be the set of bounded complex-linear maps from H to itself equipped with the Hilbert-Schmidt inner product,

texts.orig: The set of stochastic channels is manifestly convex via Eq. (12). The class of stochastic channels contains channels of known types. A mixed unitary channel is a quantum channel such that its Kraus decomposition may be taken in terms of a set of appropriately subnormalized unitary matrices. A mixed unitary channel is therefore always unital, which is to say that it leaves the maximally mixed state invariant. Indeed, some quantum channels might not appear to be mixed unitary at first sight. It turns out that question is actually NP-Hard [22]. We proceed now by way of an example that our definition of stochastic channels encompasses channels outside the mixed unitary class. Define the following 4 × 4 matrices with λ ∈ (0 , 1]
texts.text: The set of stochastic channels is manifestly convex via Eq. (12). The class of stochastic channels contains channels of known types. A mixed unitary channel is a quantum channel such that its Kraus decomposition may be taken in terms of a set of appropriately subnormalized unitary matrices. A mixed unitary channel is therefore always unital, which is to say that it leaves the maximally mixed state invariant. Indeed, some quantum channels might not appear to be mixed unitary at first sight. It turns out that question is actually NP-Hard [22]. We proceed now by way of an example that our definition of stochastic channels encompasses channels outside the mixed unitary class. Define the following 4 × 4 matrices with λ ∈ (0 , 1]

In appendix A we prove that all stochastic qubit channels are unital. We leave open the question of whether or not all stochastic channels are unital for d = 3.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: The close connection between unitary 1-designs and irreducible unitary representations (in dimension d ) [21] means that many unitary 1-designs exist. We especially recommend Bannai et al . [29] for explicit examples. The GAP system [30] could also be harvested. The Pauli group on a single qubit is a unitary 1-design. The multipartite Weyl-Heisenberg group [31] is a unitary 1-design for any collection of quantum systems. Another example is always the Clifford group [20].
texts.text: The close connection between unitary 1-designs and irreducible unitary representations (in dimension d ) [21] means that many unitary 1-designs exist. We especially recommend Bannai et al . [29] for explicit examples. The GAP system [30] could also be harvested. The Pauli group on a single qubit is a unitary 1-design. The multipartite Weyl-Heisenberg group [31] is a unitary 1-design for any collection of quantum systems. Another example is always the Clifford group [20].

texts.label: text
texts.orig: We now prove that the twirl of a quantum channel over a unitary 1-design is a stochastic quantum channel. Our proof reveals that the resulting stochastic quantum channel is noiseless with probability the process fidelity of the input channel, which underlies protocols that are designed to estimate the process fidelity [32] or suppress coherent errors [33].
texts.text: We now prove that the twirl of a quantum channel over a unitary 1-design is a stochastic quantum channel. Our proof reveals that the resulting stochastic quantum channel is noiseless with probability the process fidelity of the input channel, which underlies protocols that are designed to estimate the process fidelity [32] or suppress coherent errors [33].

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Theorem 3. Let Φ ∈ B ( B ( C d )) be quantum channel with nonzero process fidelity. Let µ be a unitary 1 -design. Then Φ µ is a stochastic channel.
texts.text: Theorem 3. Let Φ ∈ B ( B ( C d )) be quantum channel with nonzero process fidelity. Let µ be a unitary 1 -design. Then Φ µ is a stochastic channel.

texts.orig: The example defined via Eqs. (14)-(17) is not a unital stochastic channel. In light of the foregoing example and the following theorem, we conclude that some stochastic channels cannot be accessed via the twirl of any unitary 1-design.
texts.text: The example defined via Eqs. (14)-(17) is not a unital stochastic channel. In light of the foregoing example and the following theorem, we conclude that some stochastic channels cannot be accessed via the twirl of any unitary 1-design.

texts.label: section_header
texts.orig: V. CONCLUSION
texts.text: V. CONCLUSION

texts.label: text
texts.orig: We have considered properties of the class of stochastic channels. We proved that the diamond distance of a stochastic channel is its process infidelity (theorem 1). We illuminated the relationship between stochastic channels and unitary 1-designs. In general, we pointed out that channel twirling commutes with the Choi-Jamiosuppress lkowski isomorphism (lemma 2). We then demonstrated a way to access stochastic channels. The twirl of a quantum channel over a unitary 1-design is a stochastic channel (theorem 3). Next, we proved that twirling over unitary 1-designs results in unital channels (theorem 4.) We provided an example of a stochastic channel that is not unital. In appendix A, we prove that all qubit stochastic channels are unital (theorem 5).
texts.text: We have considered properties of the class of stochastic channels. We proved that the diamond distance of a stochastic channel is its process infidelity (theorem 1). We illuminated the relationship between stochastic channels and unitary 1-designs. In general, we pointed out that channel twirling commutes with the Choi-Jamiosuppress lkowski isomorphism (lemma 2). We then demonstrated a way to access stochastic channels. The twirl of a quantum channel over a unitary 1-design is a stochastic channel (theorem 3). Next, we proved that twirling over unitary 1-designs results in unital channels (theorem 4.) We provided an example of a stochastic channel that is not unital. In appendix A, we prove that all qubit stochastic channels are unital (theorem 5).

texts.label: text
texts.orig: There are some potential avenues for future research into designing stochastic channels. One open question is whether or not a quantum channel Φ exists outside the class of stochastic channels but with r ⋄ (Φ) = r (Φ). Another open question is whether or not every qutrit stochastic channel is a mixed unitary channel. Future work might also explore effects obtained via twirling over different unitary 1-designs. We observe from our proof of theorem 3 that twirling a quantum channel over any unitary 1-design results in a stochastic channel whose canonical Kraus operators can depend on the unitary 1-design in question. For instance, consider a group unitary operator basis B and then its conjugation via any element of the unitary group, Ad U ( B ). The twirl with respect to the latter is a rotated version of the former. The difference between the two twirled channels is subtle and can be missed, e.g., in [33], however, since both channels have the same diamond distance and process fidelity, the difference is typically unimportant.
texts.text: There are some potential avenues for future research into designing stochastic channels. One open question is whether or not a quantum channel Φ exists outside the class of stochastic channels but with r ⋄ (Φ) = r (Φ). Another open question is whether or not every qutrit stochastic channel is a mixed unitary channel. Future work might also explore effects obtained via twirling over different unitary 1-designs. We observe from our proof of theorem 3 that twirling a quantum channel over any unitary 1-design results in a stochastic channel whose canonical Kraus operators can depend on the unitary 1-design in question. For instance, consider a group unitary operator basis B and then its conjugation via any element of the unitary group, Ad U ( B ). The twirl with respect to the latter is a rotated version of the former. The difference between the two twirled channels is subtle and can be missed, e.g., in [33], however, since both channels have the same diamond distance and process fidelity, the difference is typically unimportant.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Finally, the set of stochastic channels is convex. A natural avenue for future research is to determine the shape of this convex set.
texts.text: Finally, the set of stochastic channels is convex. A natural avenue for future research is to determine the shape of this convex set.
